title: Lists (Read-Only)
description: Store items sequentially in a flexible way.

slides.title: A Better Container 📚
slides.content:
## The List Interface

Arrays are clunky. In modern Kotlin, we almost exclusively use **Lists** instead.

Lists provide an incredibly rich set of built-in functions to sort, search, and manipulate your data.

slides.title: Creating a List 📝
slides.content:
You create a standard List using `listOf()`.

Just like Arrays, you access elements using their Index `[ ]` starting at 0.

quizData.question: If `friends` has 3 items, what is the index of the very LAST item?
quizData.explanation: Because we start counting at 0, the items would be at indices [0], [1], and [2]. Index 3 does not exist and would cause a crash!

slides.title: Read-Only Lockdown 🔒
slides.content:
## Look, but don't touch!

Here is the catch: A standard `listOf()` in Kotlin is **Read-Only** (Immutable). 

You can read the data all day, but you cannot edit, add, or delete any items from it once it is created. Kotlin values safety above all else!

quizData.question: If you try to write `friends[0] = "Rachel"` on a standard `listOf()`, what happens?

options.text: The compiler throws an error. The list is read-only.
It adds Rachel to the end.

quizData.explanation: Standard lists (`listOf`) are permanently locked. They do not even possess an internal `.set()` or `.add()` mechanism.
